document_info.title: Timing - Expert Technical Explanations
document_info.audience: 3GPP researchers, RAN4 delegates, RRM standardization experts

7.1.2.title: UE Transmit Timing Requirements (Te timing error limits, N_TA_offset values)
7.1.2.expert_technical_overview:
The UE transmit timing requirements specified in Clause 7.1.2 establish the fundamental timing synchronization framework that enables the gNB to correctly receive uplink transmissions from geographically distributed UEs. The core principle governing this requirement is that the UE must advance its uplink frame transmission by $(N_{TA}+N_{TA offset})×T_{c}$ relative to the reception of the first detected path of the corresponding downlink frame from the reference cell. This timing advance mechanism compensates for the round-trip propagation delay between the UE and the serving cell, ensuring that uplink signals from all UEs arrive at the gNB within the prescribed receive window.

The timing error limit Te represents the maximum permissible deviation between the actual UE transmit timing and the ideal timing derived from downlink synchronization. This parameter is critically dependent on both the SCS of the SSB signals (which determines the precision of downlink timing acquisition) and the SCS of the uplink signals (which determines the granularity of uplink timing). The engineering rationale behind the Te values specified in Table 7.1.2-1 stems from the relationship between timing accuracy requirements and subcarrier spacing—higher SCS values permit tighter timing tolerances due to shorter symbol durations and correspondingly shorter cyclic prefixes. For FR2-2 operation with 960 kHz SCS, the Te limit tightens to 0.86×64×Tc, reflecting the stringent timing requirements imposed by extremely short symbol periods.

The N_TA_offset parameter serves a crucial role in multi-RAT coexistence scenarios, particularly for E-UTRA-NR and NB-IoT-NR deployments where frame structure alignment between different RATs must be maintained. The values specified in Table 7.1.2-2 (25600 Tc for standalone FR1, 0 for FR1 FDD with coexistence, 39936 for FR1 TDD with coexistence, and 13792 for FR2) are derived from the relative frame timing offsets required to prevent inter-RAT interference while maintaining backward compatibility with legacy E-UTRA deployments. The UE's ability to identify N_TA_offset through the n-TimingAdvanceOffset parameter in SIB1 or dedicated RRC signaling provides network operators with flexibility in configuring timing offsets based on deployment-specific coexistence requirements.
7.1.2.detailed_analysis:
From an implementation perspective, the UE transmit timing requirements impose significant constraints on the design of the uplink timing control loop. The initial transmission timing error requirement applies specifically to first transmissions within a DRX cycle for PUCCH, PUSCH, and SRS, as well as PRACH transmissions, msgA transmissions, and first transmissions on PSCell during SCG activation without RACH. This selective application reflects the understanding that timing accuracy is most critical at transmission boundaries where the gNB has not yet established precise timing tracking for the UE.

The SSB availability conditions specified for different UL SCS values (160 ms for SCS ≤ 120 kHz, 80 ms for 480 kHz, 40 ms for 960 kHz) establish the minimum downlink measurement window required for reliable timing acquisition. These durations are inversely proportional to the SCS, reflecting the trade-off between measurement integration time and the coherence time of the wireless channel. For high-speed scenarios or rapidly varying channel conditions, shorter SSB availability windows may necessitate more frequent timing recalibration.

The Te values exhibit a clear engineering pattern: for FR1 with 15 kHz SSB SCS and 15 kHz UL SCS, Te = 12×64×Tc (approximately 23.4 µs), while for FR2-2 with 960 kHz SSB SCS and 960 kHz UL SCS, Te = 0.86×64×Tc (approximately 1.68 µs). This reduction by a factor of ~14 reflects the inverse relationship between timing tolerance and subcarrier spacing. The intermediate values for mixed SCS configurations (e.g., 30 kHz SSB with 15 kHz UL) demonstrate the standard's accommodation of asymmetric deployments where the synchronization signal and data transmission may employ different numerologies.

The N_TA_offset parameter's dependence on coexistence scenarios addresses the practical deployment challenge of operating NR in spectrum shared with E-UTRA or NB-IoT. The value 25600 Tc for standalone FR1 corresponds to a timing offset of 0.5 ms, which aligns with the half-frame boundary in the NR frame structure. For FR1 FDD with E-UTRA-NR coexistence, N_TA_offset = 0 ensures frame boundary alignment between the two RATs, while for FR1 TDD with coexistence, N_TA_offset = 39936 (approximately 0.78 ms) provides the necessary offset to avoid cross-RAT interference in TDD configurations. The FR2 value of 13792 Tc reflects the different propagation characteristics and deployment scenarios typical of millimeter-wave operation.

The relationship between Te and N_TA_offset is multiplicative in the timing advance calculation, meaning that errors in either parameter compound in the overall timing accuracy. UE implementations must therefore maintain precise tracking of both parameters, with particular attention to the dynamic adjustment of N_TA_offset when transitioning between cells with different coexistence configurations. The specification's requirement that all UL carriers within the same TAG share the same N_TA_offset value ensures timing consistency across aggregated carriers, preventing inter-carrier interference due to timing misalignment.

key_parameters.significance: Baseline timing error limit for legacy-compatible deployments; accommodates large cell radii up to ~7 km
Optimized for eMBB deployments with high-throughput requirements; tighter timing enables higher-order modulation
Millimeter-wave deployment requirement; reflects short symbol duration and beamforming timing constraints
Ultra-tight timing for extreme SCS; enables ultra-reliable low-latency applications
Default offset aligning with NR half-frame structure; configurable via n-TimingAdvanceOffset
Frame boundary alignment for seamless E-UTRA-NR dual connectivity operation
TDD-specific offset preventing cross-RAT interference in shared spectrum deployments
Millimeter-wave specific offset optimized for small cell deployments and beam-switched operation
Fundamental NR timing granularity; all timing parameters expressed as multiples of Tc

relationships: Clause 7.1.2.1 (Gradual Timing Adjustment): Te limits bound the maximum single-step timing adjustment Tq
Clause 7.3 (Timing Advance): N_TA_offset combined with TA command determines total timing advance
TS 38.211: Defines Tc and frame structure relationships
TS 38.213 Clause 4.2: Specifies TA command application timing (k parameter)
TS 38.331: Defines n-TimingAdvanceOffset signaling

7.1.2.1.title: Gradual Timing Adjustment (Tq, Tp parameters)
7.1.2.1.expert_technical_overview:
Clause 7.1.2.1 establishes the autonomous timing adjustment mechanism that enables UEs to track timing variations arising from mobility, channel dynamics, and clock drift without requiring explicit MAC CE-based Timing Advance commands from the network. The gradual timing adjustment framework operates on three fundamental constraints: the maximum single-step adjustment magnitude Tq, the minimum aggregate adjustment rate Tp per second, and the maximum aggregate adjustment rate bounded by Tq per 200 ms (for SCS ≤ 120 kHz) or Tq per 100 ms (for SCS ≥ 480 kHz). These constraints collectively ensure that timing adjustments occur smoothly, preventing abrupt phase discontinuities that could degrade link performance or trigger false synchronization failures.

The engineering rationale for autonomous timing adjustment stems from the observation that UE mobility induces continuous propagation delay variations that, if left uncompensated, would cause uplink timing to drift outside the cyclic prefix window. Rather than burdening the network with frequent TA command transmissions, the UE is permitted to autonomously adjust its transmit timing within prescribed bounds. The Tq parameter defines the maximum permissible single-step adjustment, limiting the instantaneous phase jump that the gNB receiver must accommodate. The Tp parameter establishes the minimum tracking rate, ensuring that the UE can maintain synchronization even under rapid mobility conditions. The maximum aggregate rate constraint prevents runaway timing drift scenarios while still permitting responsive tracking of legitimate timing variations.

The Tq and Tp values specified in Table 7.1.2.1-1 exhibit a clear inverse relationship with subcarrier spacing, reflecting the tighter timing tolerances required for higher SCS deployments. For FR1 operation with SCS up to 60 kHz, both Tq and Tp are set to 5.5×64×Tc (approximately 10.75 µs), providing a balanced trade-off between tracking responsiveness and adjustment granularity. For FR2-1, the introduction of the K parameter (K = 2.5 or 4.5 depending on highSpeedMeasFlagFR2-r17 configuration) enables differentiated behavior for high-speed scenarios, with the elevated K value permitting larger single-step adjustments to accommodate rapid timing variations induced by high-velocity mobility. FR2-2 operation further tightens these limits to 2.5×64×Tc for 120 kHz SCS and 0.8×64×Tc for 480/960 kHz SCS, reflecting the extreme timing precision requirements of ultra-high-frequency deployments.
7.1.2.1.detailed_analysis:
The autonomous timing adjustment mechanism specified in Clause 7.1.2.1 operates independently of the MAC CE-based Timing Advance procedure defined in Clause 7.3, creating a dual-layer timing control architecture. The MAC CE-based procedure handles large timing corrections (e.g., at handover or after DRX cycles) and coarse timing acquisition, while the autonomous adjustment mechanism provides fine-grained continuous tracking during active transmission periods. This architectural separation optimizes signaling overhead—autonomous adjustments require no network interaction, while MAC CE commands are reserved for scenarios exceeding the autonomous adjustment capabilities.

The maximum aggregate adjustment rate constraint (Tq per 200 ms or 100 ms) serves a critical purpose in preventing timing instability. Without this constraint, a UE could theoretically accumulate large timing adjustments over extended periods, potentially causing the actual timing to diverge significantly from the network's expectation based on the last TA command. The rate limit ensures that any accumulated timing drift is bounded and that the network's TA command remains valid within predictable limits. For high-SCS deployments (≥ 480 kHz), the more stringent 100 ms window reflects the reduced tolerance for timing drift associated with shorter cyclic prefixes.

The K parameter introduced for FR2-1 represents an important enhancement for high-speed mobility scenarios. When highSpeedMeasFlagFR2-r17 is configured for power class 6 UEs, K = 4.5 enables single-step adjustments of 4.5×64×Tc (approximately 14.68 µs), compared to the baseline 2.5×64×Tc (approximately 8.16 µs) for normal operation. This 80% increase in maximum step size directly addresses the challenge of tracking rapid timing variations in high-speed train or vehicular scenarios where the propagation delay can change by several microseconds per second. The conditional application of the elevated K value ensures that the relaxed timing constraints are only invoked when explicitly configured, preserving the tighter baseline requirements for general deployments.

Implementation of the gradual timing adjustment mechanism requires careful consideration of the interaction between the UE's internal timing control loop and the autonomous adjustment constraints. The UE must maintain a timing error estimator that continuously evaluates the deviation between the current transmit timing and the ideal timing derived from downlink measurements. When this error exceeds the autonomous adjustment threshold (implicitly defined by the Tq constraint), the UE applies a correction limited to Tq magnitude. The timing of these adjustments must respect the minimum rate Tp, ensuring that the UE does not defer necessary corrections indefinitely.

The relationship between Tq and the Te timing error limit from Clause 7.1.2 is noteworthy: Tq values are generally smaller than Te limits, reflecting the distinction between single-step adjustment capability (Tq) and cumulative timing accuracy (Te). For example, in FR1 with 15 kHz SCS, Te = 12×64×Tc while Tq = 5.5×64×Tc, meaning that a single autonomous adjustment cannot fully correct a timing error at the Te boundary—multiple adjustments or a MAC CE-based TA command would be required. This design ensures that timing corrections are applied gradually, minimizing disruption to ongoing transmissions.

The minimum aggregate adjustment rate Tp ensures that the UE maintains proactive timing tracking rather than reactive correction. By requiring adjustments of at least Tp magnitude per second (when timing variation is present), the specification prevents scenarios where the UE defers timing corrections until they become critical. This proactive behavior is essential for maintaining link quality during mobility, as delayed timing corrections can cause cumulative degradation in reception quality at the gNB.

key_parameters.significance: Maximum single-step autonomous adjustment; balances tracking responsiveness with phase continuity
Minimum aggregate adjustment rate ensuring proactive timing tracking
Baseline FR2 single-step limit; tighter than FR1 due to shorter symbol duration
Elevated limit for high-speed mobility scenarios (K=4.5 with highSpeedMeasFlagFR2-r17)
Minimum tracking rate for FR2 deployments; independent of high-speed flag
Ultra-high-frequency adjustment limit for 120 kHz numerology
Extreme precision requirement for highest SCS deployments
Prevents runaway timing drift while permitting responsive tracking
Enhanced rate limit for high-SCS deployments with reduced timing tolerance

relationships: Clause 7.1.2: Tq values derived from Te timing error limits
Clause 7.3: Autonomous adjustment complements MAC CE-based TA commands
Clause 7.1.2.3: One-shot large UL timing adjustment overrides gradual limits for high-speed PC6 UEs
TS 38.213: Timing adjustment application synchronized with slot boundaries

7.3.title: Timing Advance (adjustment delay and accuracy)
7.3.expert_technical_overview:
Clause 7.3 defines the network-controlled Timing Advance (TA) mechanism, which represents the primary interface between the gNB and UE for coarse timing synchronization. Unlike the autonomous adjustment mechanism in Clause 7.1.2.1, the TA procedure operates through explicit MAC CE commands from the network, enabling the gNB to directly control UE transmit timing based on its reception of uplink signals. The TA command conveys a timing adjustment value (N_TA) that the UE applies to its transmit timing, with the total timing advance given by $(N_{TA}+N_{TA offset})×T_{c}$ as defined in Clause 7.1.2.

The adjustment delay requirement specified in Clause 7.3.2.1 establishes the timing relationship between TA command reception and adjustment application. The UE shall adjust its uplink transmission timing at slot n+k+1 for a TA command received in slot n, where k is defined in Clause 4.2 of TS 38.213. This deterministic delay ensures that both the UE and gNB have a common understanding of when timing changes take effect, enabling the gNB to predict UE transmit timing for scheduling decisions. The k parameter accounts for UE processing time, including MAC CE decoding, timing calculation, and hardware reconfiguration.

The adjustment accuracy requirements in Clause 7.3.2.2 specify the maximum permissible deviation between the commanded timing adjustment and the actual timing change implemented by the UE. These accuracy requirements are directly tied to subcarrier spacing, with tighter tolerances for higher SCS deployments. The progression from ±256 Tc for 15/30 kHz SCS to ±6 Tc for 960 kHz SCS reflects the inverse relationship between timing accuracy requirements and symbol duration. At 960 kHz SCS, where the symbol period is approximately 1.04 µs, a ±6 Tc accuracy (approximately ±3.06 µs) represents a substantial fraction of the cyclic prefix, emphasizing the critical importance of precise timing control at extreme SCS values.
7.3.detailed_analysis:
The Timing Advance mechanism serves as the foundation for uplink synchronization in NR, building upon the principles established in LTE while adapting to the multi-numerology framework of 5G. The TA command, conveyed through the MAC CE as specified in TS 38.321, provides 6-bit or 12-bit timing adjustment values corresponding to N_TA increments. The 6-bit format (TA index 0-63) provides coarse adjustments of 16×64×Tc per step, while the 12-bit format enables finer granularity for scenarios requiring precise timing control.

The adjustment delay specification (slot n+k+1) creates a predictable timing control loop that the gNB can exploit for scheduling optimization. The k parameter, defined in TS 38.213 Clause 4.2, accounts for the processing delay between MAC CE reception and timing adjustment application. For FDD operation, k is typically 6 slots for 15 kHz SCS, scaling inversely with SCS (k = 6×15/SCS_kHz). This scaling ensures consistent processing time across different numerologies. The deterministic nature of this delay enables the gNB to issue TA commands in advance of when the adjustment is needed, compensating for the control loop latency.

The adjustment accuracy requirements exhibit a clear engineering progression tied to the fundamental timing characteristics of each SCS. At 15 kHz SCS, where the cyclic prefix duration is approximately 4.7 µs, the ±256 Tc accuracy (approximately ±130 µs) appears generous relative to the cyclic prefix. However, this tolerance must be understood in the context of the overall timing error budget, which includes contributions from UE measurement error, propagation estimation uncertainty, and TA command quantization. The ±256 Tc accuracy ensures that the TA mechanism itself does not dominate the error budget.

For higher SCS values, the accuracy requirements tighten proportionally. At 60 kHz SCS, the requirement tightens to ±128 Tc (approximately ±65 µs), reflecting the halved cyclic prefix duration. At 120 kHz SCS, the ±32 Tc requirement (approximately ±16 µs) represents a significant tightening, acknowledging the reduced tolerance for timing errors in millimeter-wave deployments where beam misalignment can compound timing uncertainties. The extreme accuracy requirements for 480 kHz (±10 Tc ≈ 5.1 µs) and 960 kHz (±6 Tc ≈ 3.1 µs) reflect the ultra-short symbol durations in FR2-2, where timing errors consume an increasingly significant fraction of the available cyclic prefix.

The relationship between TA adjustment accuracy and the Te timing error limit from Clause 7.1.2 is multiplicative: the UE must maintain Te-level timing accuracy even after applying TA adjustments. This implies that the UE's timing control loop must account for both the commanded adjustment and the inherent adjustment error. For example, when applying a TA command at 15 kHz SCS, the UE must ensure that the actual timing change is within ±256 Tc of the commanded value, while the resulting absolute timing error must remain within the Te limit of 12×64×Tc (for 15 kHz SSB/UL configuration).

Implementation of the TA mechanism requires careful coordination between the MAC layer (which processes the TA command), the PHY layer (which implements the timing adjustment), and the RF subsystem (which provides the actual timing reference). The adjustment delay specification provides a well-defined interface between these layers, with the MAC layer responsible for interpreting the TA command and scheduling the adjustment, and the PHY layer responsible for applying the timing change at the specified slot boundary. The accuracy requirements impose constraints on the PHY layer implementation, particularly the design of the timing control loop and the precision of the RF timing reference.

The TA mechanism interacts with the autonomous adjustment mechanism in Clause 7.1.2.1 in a hierarchical manner: TA commands provide coarse timing corrections, while autonomous adjustments provide fine-grained tracking. When a TA command is applied, the UE's internal timing error estimator should be reset to reflect the new timing reference, and subsequent autonomous adjustments should track variations relative to this new reference. This hierarchical approach optimizes the trade-off between signaling overhead (minimized by autonomous adjustments) and correction range (maximized by TA commands).

key_parameters.significance: Deterministic timing for TA command application; k defined per SCS in TS 38.213
Coarse accuracy for legacy-compatible deployments; sufficient for large-cell operation
Same accuracy as 15 kHz; processing time scales with SCS
Tighter accuracy reflecting halved cyclic prefix duration
Millimeter-wave deployment requirement; 4× tighter than 60 kHz
Extreme precision for FR2-2 high-bandwidth operation
Ultra-tight accuracy for highest SCS; ~2% of 960 kHz cyclic prefix
Coarse adjustment step size; 63 steps provide ~0.5 ms total range
Extended range and resolution for demanding scenarios

relationships: Clause 7.1.2: N_TA from TA command combined with N_TA_offset for total timing advance
Clause 7.1.2.1: TA commands provide coarse corrections; autonomous adjustments provide fine tracking
TS 38.321: Defines MAC CE format for TA command transmission
TS 38.213 Clause 4.2: Specifies k parameter for adjustment delay
TS 38.211: Defines N_TA calculation and Tc timing unit

7.5.title: Maximum Transmission Timing Difference (EN-DC, NR-CA, NR-DC scenarios)
7.5.expert_technical_overview:
Clause 7.5 addresses the critical challenge of maintaining uplink timing synchronization in multi-connectivity and carrier aggregation scenarios where the UE must transmit to multiple cells or on multiple carriers with potentially different timing references. The maximum transmission timing difference requirements define the UE's capability to handle relative timing offsets between the subframe/slot boundaries of different cells, ensuring that the UE can maintain parallel uplink transmissions without excessive timing misalignment that would degrade reception quality at any of the receiving cells.

The requirements are structured around different deployment scenarios: inter-band EN-DC (E-UTRA-NR Dual Connectivity), intra-band EN-DC, NR Carrier Aggregation (NR-CA), inter-band NE-DC (NR-E-UTRA Dual Connectivity), and inter-band NR-DC (NR-NR Dual Connectivity). Each scenario presents unique timing challenges based on the frequency ranges involved, the duplexing modes of the component carriers, and the synchronization relationships between the cells. The timing difference requirements are expressed in microseconds, representing the maximum permissible offset between the transmission timing references of the involved cells.

For asynchronous EN-DC scenarios (Clause 7.5.2), the maximum timing difference scales inversely with the subcarrier spacing of the PSCell, reflecting the relationship between timing tolerance and symbol duration. The values range from 500 µs for 15 kHz SCS to 62.5 µs for 120 kHz SCS, demonstrating the stringent timing requirements for high-SCS millimeter-wave operation. Synchronous EN-DC scenarios (Clause 7.5.2.1) feature a uniform 35.21 µs requirement across all SCS configurations, reflecting the tighter synchronization achieved through network timing alignment. Intra-band synchronous EN-DC (Clause 7.5.3) further tightens the requirement to 5.21 µs, leveraging the shared frequency band for enhanced timing coherence.
7.5.detailed_analysis:
The engineering foundation of Clause 7.5 lies in the recognition that multi-connectivity scenarios introduce timing complexity beyond single-cell operation. When the UE transmits to multiple cells, each cell has its own timing reference based on the propagation delay to that cell. In EN-DC scenarios, the E-UTRA PCell and NR PSCell may have different timing references, requiring the UE to maintain parallel transmit timing adjustments. The maximum transmission timing difference requirements define the UE's capability boundary for handling these parallel timing references.

For inter-band asynchronous EN-DC (Table 7.5.2-1), the timing difference requirement of 500 µs for 15 kHz SCS corresponds to approximately 7.5 km difference in propagation distance, accommodating scenarios where the E-UTRA and NR cells are geographically separated. As the PSCell SCS increases, the timing difference requirement tightens proportionally: 250 µs for 30 kHz, 125 µs for 60 kHz, and 62.5 µs for 120 kHz. This inverse scaling reflects the reduced tolerance for timing misalignment at higher SCS values, where the shorter cyclic prefix provides less margin for timing errors. The 62.5 µs limit for 120 kHz SCS represents approximately 18.75 km propagation distance difference, still accommodating significant geographic separation.

The synchronous EN-DC requirements (Table 7.5.2.1-1) feature a uniform 35.21 µs limit across all SCS configurations, reflecting the assumption that network synchronization (e.g., through GPS or IEEE 1588) aligns the cell timings to within a tight tolerance. This uniform requirement simplifies UE implementation by decoupling the timing difference capability from the SCS configuration. The 35.21 µs value is derived from network synchronization accuracy specifications and provides margin for propagation delay differences within a typical deployment area.

Intra-band synchronous EN-DC (Table 7.5.3-1) features the tightest requirement of 5.21 µs, reflecting the enhanced timing coherence achievable when both cells operate in the same frequency band. The intra-band scenario benefits from similar propagation characteristics and enables tighter timing alignment. Note 2 in this table provides important guidance: if the transmission timing difference exceeds the cyclic prefix length of the PSCell UL SCS, NR UE Tx EVM degradation is expected for symbols overlapping the LTE subframe boundary. This note highlights the practical consequence of timing misalignment—inter-symbol interference due to cyclic prefix violation.

NR Carrier Aggregation requirements (Table 7.5.4-1) address timing differences between Timing Advance Groups (TAGs) in CA scenarios. The requirements are specified per frequency range pair: 34.6 µs for FR1-FR1, 8.5 µs for FR2-1-FR2-1, 26.1 µs for FR1-FR2-1, and 26.1 µs for FR1-FR2-2. These values reflect the different propagation characteristics and deployment scenarios typical of each frequency range combination. The tighter 8.5 µs requirement for FR2-1 CA reflects the smaller cell sizes and tighter timing tolerances of millimeter-wave deployments.

Inter-band NE-DC (Table 7.5.5-1) mirrors the EN-DC structure but with reversed RAT roles: the NR PCell and E-UTRA PSCell. The timing difference requirements follow the same inverse-SCS scaling: 500 µs for 15 kHz, 250 µs for 30 kHz, 125 µs for 60 kHz, and 62.5 µs for 120 kHz. Synchronous NE-DC (Table 7.5.5.1-1) features the same 35.21 µs uniform requirement as synchronous EN-DC, reflecting the common underlying network synchronization assumption.

Inter-band NR-DC (Tables 7.5.6-1 and 7.5.6-2) addresses dual-connectivity scenarios between two NR cells, potentially in different frequency ranges. Synchronous NR-DC requirements (Table 7.5.6-1) specify 34.6 µs for FR1-FR1, 8.5 µs for FR2-1-FR2-1, 34.1 µs for FR1-FR2-1, and 34.1 µs for FR1-FR2-2. Asynchronous NR-DC (Table 7.5.6-2) features the same inverse-SCS scaling as asynchronous EN-DC/NE-DC, with the addition of requirements for extreme SCS values: 15.625 µs for 480 kHz and 7.8125 µs for 960 kHz. These ultra-tight requirements reflect the minimal timing tolerance of FR2-2 deployments.

Multi-TRP requirements (Tables 7.5.7-1 and 7.5.7-2) address scenarios where the UE transmits to multiple transmission/reception points (TRPs) within the same cell. For UEs not supporting rxTimingDiff-r18, the requirement is CP length plus 1.6 µs for FR1 and CP length plus 0.5 µs for FR2-1, ensuring that timing differences do not exceed the cyclic prefix plus a small margin. For UEs supporting rxTimingDiff-r18, the requirements align with inter-band CA: 34.6 µs for FR1 and 8.5 µs for FR2-1.

key_parameters.parameter: Max TX timing diff (async EN-DC, 120 kHz)

key_parameters.parameter: Max TX timing diff (intra-band sync EN-DC)
key_parameters.significance: Tightest EN-DC requirement; shared band enables precise timing
Inter-band CA between FR1 carriers; similar to sync EN-DC
mmWave CA; tight limit reflecting small cell geometry

key_parameters.parameter: Max TX timing diff (async NR-DC, 960 kHz)

key_parameters.parameter: Max TX timing diff (multi-TRP FR1, no rxTimingDiff-r18)

7.6.title: Maximum Receive Timing Difference
7.6.expert_technical_overview:
Clause 7.6 complements the transmission timing difference requirements of Clause 7.5 by specifying the UE's capability to handle relative receive timing differences between downlink signals from multiple cells in multi-connectivity and carrier aggregation scenarios. While Clause 7.5 addresses the UE's ability to transmit with appropriate timing to multiple cells, Clause 7.6 addresses the UE's ability to receive and process downlink signals that arrive at different times due to propagation delay differences between the cells. The maximum receive timing difference requirements ensure that the UE can maintain coherent reception across all aggregated cells without excessive buffer requirements or processing complexity.

The receive timing difference is defined as the relative timing offset between the subframe timing boundary of an E-UTRA cell (in EN-DC/NE-DC scenarios) or the slot timing boundary of an NR cell and the corresponding boundary of another cell in the aggregated configuration. This timing difference arises from the geographic separation between cells and the resulting differences in propagation delay to the UE location. The requirements are expressed in microseconds, representing the maximum timing offset that the UE must be capable of handling while maintaining specified reception performance.

The structure of Clause 7.6 mirrors that of Clause 7.5, with requirements organized by deployment scenario: inter-band EN-DC (asynchronous and synchronous), intra-band EN-DC, NR Carrier Aggregation (intra-band non-contiguous and inter-band), inter-band NE-DC (asynchronous and synchronous), inter-band NR-DC (synchronous and asynchronous), PC6 UE in FR2, and multi-TRP scenarios. This parallel structure reflects the complementary nature of transmit and receive timing requirements in multi-connectivity operation.
7.6.detailed_analysis:
The engineering basis for receive timing difference requirements stems from the UE's internal signal processing architecture. When receiving from multiple cells, the UE must align the received signals in time for coherent combining (in CA scenarios) or independent processing (in DC scenarios). The receive timing difference determines the size of the buffer required to store early-arriving signals while waiting for later-arriving signals, as well as the complexity of the timing alignment processing. Excessive timing differences can exceed the UE's buffering capability or introduce unacceptable processing latency.

For inter-band asynchronous EN-DC (Table 7.6.2-1), the receive timing difference requirements mirror the transmission timing difference requirements, ranging from 500 µs for 15 kHz SCS to 62.5 µs for 120 kHz SCS. This symmetry reflects the underlying physical principle that propagation delay differences affect both uplink and downlink equally. The DL subcarrier spacing used for determining the requirement is defined as min{SCS_SSB, SCS_DATA}, ensuring that the requirement accounts for the most timing-critical signal (typically the SSB due to its role in cell synchronization). Synchronous EN-DC (Table 7.6.2.1-1) features a uniform 33 µs requirement, slightly tighter than the corresponding 35.21 µs transmit timing requirement, reflecting the potentially more stringent processing requirements for reception.

Intra-band synchronous EN-DC (Table 7.6.3-1) features the tightest requirement of 3 µs, significantly tighter than the 5.21 µs transmit timing requirement. This asymmetry reflects the different processing challenges of reception versus transmission: while transmission timing can be adjusted through the TA mechanism, reception timing must be handled through buffering and alignment processing, which becomes increasingly complex as timing differences grow. The 3 µs requirement ensures that intra-band EN-DC can be supported with modest buffer sizes and processing complexity. Note 2 indicates that this requirement also applies to intra-band synchronous NE-DC, maintaining consistency across dual connectivity modes.

NR Carrier Aggregation requirements address both intra-band non-contiguous (Table 7.6.4-1) and inter-band (Table 7.6.4-2) scenarios. Intra-band non-contiguous CA features requirements of 31 µs for FR1 and 0.26 µs for FR2-1, with Note 1 providing important guidance: if the receive time difference exceeds the cyclic prefix length of a particular SCS, demodulation performance degradation is expected for the first symbol of the slot. This note explicitly links the receive timing difference requirement to demodulation performance, highlighting the practical consequence of excessive timing differences. The extremely tight 0.26 µs requirement for FR2-1 intra-band non-contiguous CA reflects the minimal timing tolerance of millimeter-wave deployments with very small cell sizes.

Inter-band NR CA (Table 7.6.4-2) specifies requirements of 33 µs for FR1-FR1, 8 µs for FR2-1-FR2-1, 25 µs for FR1-FR2-1, and 25 µs for FR1-FR2-2. These values are comparable to the corresponding transmission timing difference requirements, with slight variations reflecting the different processing requirements of reception. The 8 µs requirement for FR2-1 CA is slightly tighter than the 8.5 µs transmit timing requirement, consistent with the pattern of slightly tighter receive requirements observed in other scenarios.

Inter-band NE-DC (Table 7.6.5-1) follows the same inverse-SCS scaling as EN-DC: 500 µs for 15 kHz, 250 µs for 30 kHz, 125 µs for 60 kHz, and 62.5 µs for 120 kHz. Synchronous NE-DC (Table 7.6.5.1-1) features a uniform 33 µs requirement, matching the synchronous EN-DC receive timing requirement. The symmetry between EN-DC and NE-DC requirements reflects the fundamental similarity of the underlying timing challenges, regardless of which RAT serves as PCell versus PSCell.

Inter-band NR-DC (Tables 7.6.6-1 and 7.6.6-2) addresses dual-connectivity between two NR cells. Synchronous NR-DC (Table 7.6.6-1) specifies 33 µs for FR1-FR1, 8 µs for FR2-1-FR2-1, 33 µs for FR1-FR2-1, and 33 µs for FR1-FR2-2. Notably, the FR1-FR2-1 and FR1-FR2-2 requirements match the FR1-FR1 requirement rather than following the intermediate pattern observed in transmission timing differences. This design choice simplifies UE implementation by avoiding intermediate timing difference requirements for cross-FR DC scenarios. Asynchronous NR-DC (Table 7.6.6-2) follows the same inverse-SCS scaling as other asynchronous scenarios, with the addition of extreme SCS requirements: 15.625 µs for 480 kHz and 7.8125 µs for 960 kHz.

PC6 UE in FR2 (Table 7.6.7-1) introduces a specialized requirement of 8 µs for PC6 UEs supporting simultaneousReceptionTwoQCL-r18 at 120 kHz SCS in FR2-1. This requirement addresses the specific timing challenges of simultaneous reception from two quasi-co-located (QCL) sources, which may be relevant for advanced beamforming scenarios or multi-TRP reception.

Multi-TRP requirements (Tables 7.6.8-1, 7.6.8-2, and 7.6.8-3) address receive timing differences in multi-TRP scenarios. For UEs not supporting rxTimingDiff-r18 (Table 7.6.8-1), the requirement is simply the cyclic prefix length of the maximum SCS on the carrier, ensuring that timing differences do not exceed the natural tolerance provided by the cyclic prefix. For UEs supporting rxTimingDiff-r18 (Table 7.6.8-2), the requirements align with inter-band CA: 33 µs for FR1 and 8 µs for FR2-1. Multi-RX capable UEs (Table 7.6.8-3) have a requirement of cyclic prefix length for FR2-1, reflecting the enhanced reception capabilities of such UEs.

key_parameters.parameter: Max RX timing diff (async EN-DC, 15 kHz)
key_parameters.significance: Baseline for legacy deployments; accommodates large cell separation

key_parameters.parameter: Max RX timing diff (async EN-DC, 120 kHz)
key_parameters.significance: mmWave limit; inverse scaling with SCS
Slightly tighter than TX requirement (35.21 µs); reflects RX processing complexity

key_parameters.parameter: Max RX timing diff (intra-band sync EN-DC)
key_parameters.significance: Tightest requirement; significantly tighter than TX (5.21 µs)

key_parameters.parameter: Max RX timing diff (NR-CA intra-band non-contiguous FR1)
key_parameters.significance: Non-contiguous CA within FR1; comparable to sync EN-DC

key_parameters.parameter: Max RX timing diff (NR-CA intra-band non-contiguous FR2-1)
key_parameters.significance: Extremely tight for mmWave; Note 1 links to CP and demodulation degradation

key_parameters.parameter: Max RX timing diff (NR-CA inter-band FR2-1-FR2-1)
key_parameters.significance: mmWave inter-band CA; slightly tighter than TX (8.5 µs)

key_parameters.parameter: Max RX timing diff (async NR-DC, 960 kHz)
key_parameters.significance: Extreme SCS requirement; matches TX timing diff

key_parameters.parameter: Max RX timing diff (multi-TRP FR1, no rxTimingDiff-r18)
key_parameters.significance: Natural cyclic prefix tolerance for basic multi-TRP

key_parameters.parameter: Max RX timing diff (multi-TRP FR1, with rxTimingDiff-r18)
key_parameters.significance: Enhanced capability enables relaxed timing requirement
